problem_summary: 2013 MCM Problem A (continuous): the "Ultimate Brownie Pan." Rectangular pans overcook food at the corners because heat concentrates there; rounded/circular pans heat evenly but waste oven rack space. The team had to (1) explain/model heat distribution along pan edges as a function of pan shape, and (2) design an optimal pan shape and rack arrangement that trades off the number of pans fitting in the oven (N) against evenness of heating (H), with user weights p and (1−p), and show how the optimum varies with oven width/length ratio W/L and p.
models_used: Model 1 — Steady-state heat-conduction PDE with convective boundary: starts from the transient Fourier heat equation ρc·∂T/∂t − λ∇²T = Q_g, sets ∂T/∂t = 0 (t→∞, balance) to get steady-state −λ∇²T = h(T_ext − T), a Helmholtz-type equation with a convective (Robin-flavored) source term
Neumann boundary condition q_edge = −λ ∂T/∂n = q_0 (prescribed normal heat flux on edges) — uniform flux into the metal from the bottom/sides
3D→2D dimensional reduction: assumes bottom heating is uniform so unevenness comes only from side facets, collapsing the 3D box to a 2D polygon cross-section
Finite Element Method (FEM) numerical solution via MATLAB PDE Toolbox to compute the steady-state temperature field on square, polygon, and circular pans

Curve fitting (MATLAB) of edge-temperature standard deviation σ vs. number of edges m, yielding the empirical law σ ≈ 23.605/m² + 0.035 ∝ 1/m² (edge-temperature non-uniformity falls off as the inverse square of the edge count)

Model 2 — Bi-objective pan/rack optimization: minimize a weighted objective min[R·η·p + σ·(1−p)] combining area-utilization loss η = 1 − NA/(2S) and a per-unit temperature-discrepancy rate σ = (Σ nᵢσᵢ)/(σ₀N), normalized against the rectangular pan's σ₀, with a correction factor R to equalize the two factors' rates of change
Integer programming formulation for the packing/selection: decision vector n = (n₄, n₆, n₈, n_∞) with Σnᵢ = N, nᵢ ∈ Z₊, constraint 2S − NA ≥ 0, limited to at most two pan shapes per rack
Closed-form rectangular close-packing bound N_max = 2⌊S/A⌋ for the p=1 (max-N) corner case

2-D tessellation / plane-tiling argument (only rectangle and regular hexagon tile the plane gap-free; octagons leave interstitial holes; m≥12 polygons approximated by circles) to prune the candidate shape set

Custom 'Center Dot Matrix Statistics' arrangement algorithm — overlays the rack with the lattice of polygon centers, then counts how many centers satisfy clearance conditions (distance to each rack edge ≥ inscribed-circle radius, ≥ circumcircle radius for hexagons), with a 90° rack rotation re-check for hexagons, to compute the maximum packable count per shape
Sensitivity analysis on p and W/L revealing discrete 'sudden jump' behavior of the integer-programming optimum at boundary points
key_techniques: Reducing an intractable 3D transient PDE to a solvable 2D steady-state BVP by (a) taking t→∞ to kill the time derivative and (b) arguing bottom heating is uniform so only side facets drive non-uniformity — a justified, clearly-stated simplification chain
Offloading the PDE solve to MATLAB PDE Toolbox FEM instead of seeking an analytic solution, then extracting a single scalar (edge-temperature σ) from each field as the usable physics output
Turning simulation output into a clean empirical scaling law σ ∝ 1/m² via curve fitting — converts a numerical experiment into a closed-form ingredient that feeds Model 2

Defining two commensurable, normalized objectives (area-utilization loss η and per-unit discrepancy σ₀-normalized) so a single weighted sum is meaningful, plus a correction factor R that matches the two factors' rates of change so the weight p behaves intuitively
Using plane-tiling theory to drastically shrink the search space to {rectangle, hexagon, octagon, circle} before any optimization — domain insight as a pruning step

Case decomposition by p: p=1 (pure max-N → rectangles, closed form), p=0 (pure even-heat → circles), 0<p<1 (mixed integer program) — maps the three problem conditions onto three tractable sub-solutions
Inventing a discrete lattice-counting algorithm (center dot matrix) to answer 'how many shapes fit in a finite bounded rack', with explicit geometric admissibility tests and a flowchart

Real-world grounding: instantiating with an actual Hamilton Beach oven (9"×13" racks, 754.84 cm²) and 30 cm² brownies, running three concrete tests that show the optimum shape flips (hexagon→octagon) with small changes in p or W/L
Explicitly reporting the sensitivity 'discontinuity' as a feature/risk (integer optima jump at boundary W/L) and giving a design recommendation to avoid boundary values
why_it_won: "It nailed the two things judges reward most on a continuous Problem A: a physically-grounded mechanism and a clean end-to-end pipeline. Rather than hand-waving about corners, it derived overcooking from the heat equation + convective BC, solved it with FEM, and distilled the result into a memorable closed-form law (σ ∝ 1/m²) that directly motivates 'more edges = more even.' That law then became a reusable building block in the optimization stage, so the two models are genuinely coupled rather than bolted together. The team then did the hard, distinctive thing: it actually solved the packing problem in a finite bounded rack with a self-invented, clearly-specified algorithm (Center Dot Matrix Statistics), instead of stopping at infinite-plane tiling. It addressed all three required conditions (max N, max evenness, weighted combo) explicitly, validated on a real named oven with real numbers, showed the optimum shape changing with p and W/L (demonstrating the model is responsive, not trivial), and ran a sensitivity analysis that surfaced and explained a genuine discontinuity. Finally it delivered the required brochure/advertising sheet with consumer-facing framing ('crisp corners vs. soft even bakes'), closing the modeling-to-communication loop the problem demands."
section_highlights: "Structure is textbook-clean and judge-friendly: Summary → Introduction (with an explicit 4-step decomposition of the solution plan) → numbered Basic Assumptions + a full notation table → Model 1 (physics) → Model 2 (optimization) → Algorithm (with flowchart) → Model Test (three concrete cases on a real oven) → Sensitivity Analysis → Strengths/Weaknesses per model → Improvement → Advertising Sheet → References. Strong moves: (1) the Summary previews exactly what was built ('two models and one algorithm') and the key finding; (2) every modeling choice is preceded by an explicit, numbered assumption; (3) Model 1's physics output (σ ∝ 1/m²) is visually supported by a deviation table across m=4…16,∞ and a curve-fit plot, then explicitly reused in Model 2's objective; (4) the algorithm is given as prose conditions AND a flowchart for reproducibility; (5) the three test cases each show the resulting arrangement figure and the numerical objective value, demonstrating the model 'moves' (hexagon at p=0.5/WL=0.69, octagon at p=0.25, octagon again when WL nudges to 0.683); (6) per-model Strengths/Weaknesses honesty plus an Improvement section (rounded corners, odd-edge polygons); (7) the brochure translates math into customer language. Weak spots in writing: noticeable English/typo errors (garbled symbols like '??', 'clos package', 'normnot polygon'), and some figures' extracted data tables look inconsistent — but the logical scaffolding is very strong."
pitfalls_or_limitations: 3D→2D reduction is a real physical approximation: the team acknowledges the side-facet-only assumption means the computed field is not the true heat distribution; corner 3D effects and bottom-conduction gradients are discarded

The steady-state assumption (t→∞) ignores the actual baking transient — real overcooking is a time-integrated thermal dose, not just the equilibrium field; doneness/browning chemistry (Maillard, moisture) is never modeled

Heat-transfer parameters (λ=50, h=100, T_ext=330 K, q_0=1000) are asserted as a 'standard state' with no calibration to a real pan or experiment, and radiation is dropped despite being listed as one of three transfer modes

The correction factor R is hand-waved to a constant (R=const, then set to 1 in tests) via a loose 'changing speeds are approximately equal, opposite direction' argument — this is the weakest link, since R directly scales how p trades off the two objectives and the result (which shape wins) depends on it

Search space is artificially restricted: odd-edge regular polygons (pentagon, heptagon) are excluded, m≥12 are forced to circles, and at most two shapes per rack are allowed — so the reported 'optimum' is optimal only within a pruned discrete menu
η = 1 − NA/(2S): the factor of 2 (two racks) is baked into the utilization definition, making the metric oven-specific and slightly opaque

The integer-programming optimum is discontinuous in W/L and p (their own sensitivity finding); a 0.01 change in W/L flips the optimal shape, so the design is fragile near boundary values and the 'optimal' label is brittle

Some figure data tables in the document appear noisy/inconsistent (e.g., the Fig. 3/Fig. 6 extracted values), and there are pervasive English/typographical errors that would slightly hurt a borderline paper
No uncertainty/error quantification on the FEM solution (mesh convergence, BC sensitivity) and no validation of σ ∝ 1/m² against any independent data
reusable_patterns: 'Physics model feeds optimization model' coupling: solve a governing PDE, extract one scalar metric (here edge-temperature σ), fit it to a closed-form law (σ ∝ 1/m²), then plug that law in as a term of a downstream objective function — keeps the two halves genuinely integrated

Steady-state + dimensional-reduction recipe to make a PDE tractable: kill the time derivative via t→∞ equilibrium, then argue one dimension/face dominates the variation to drop from 3D to 2D, always stating the assumption explicitly and listing it as a named weakness
Use a general-purpose solver (MATLAB PDE Toolbox FEM) as a black box for the hard continuous part, and spend your originality budget on a custom discrete algorithm for the part no library solves

Normalize competing objectives to comparable per-unit scales and add an explicit correction/weight factor so a single weighted-sum objective with a user weight p is interpretable — and discuss how to set that factor

Domain-theory pruning before optimization: use a structural fact (only rectangle & hexagon tile the plane) to collapse an infinite design space to a handful of candidates, then brute-force/IP over the small set

Corner-case decomposition by the trade-off weight: solve the extremes (p=0 and p=1) in closed form first, then handle the interior 0<p<1 with the general integer program — gives sanity checks and bounds

Lattice/center-counting algorithm for 'how many congruent shapes fit in a finite bounded region': lay down the center lattice, test each candidate center against geometric clearance conditions, rotate and re-test for anisotropic lattices, count — a generic finite-domain packing heuristic worth keeping in a template library

Validate on a real, named instance with real numbers (actual oven dimensions, realistic food area) and run multiple parameter cases to demonstrate the optimum is responsive (shape flips with p and W/L), not a degenerate constant
Always run a sensitivity sweep on the key parameters and report discontinuities honestly as both a finding and a design caution
Close the loop with the deliverable the prompt asks for (here a consumer brochure) translating the math into the audience's language — judges weight communication explicitly
